title: Truth, Fiction, and a Hollywood Heist Movie, With Bart Layton
format: Sean Fennessey talks to Bart Layton about 'American Animals,' the true story of a college heist gone wrong, and why he built the film out of both actors and the real men who committed the crime.

films.title: American Animals
films.note: Layton's fiction debut, about four college students who planned an elaborate heist of rare books from their university library. He cast Evan Peters and Barry Keoghan as leads, then wove in the real men recounting the crime documentary-style, rewriting the script after they wouldn't say his scripted lines verbatim. Premiered at Sundance; The Orchard and MoviePass co-distributed it.

films.title: The Imposter
films.note: Layton's documentary debut. Two of the real subjects had seen it before he wrote to them in prison, which helped him win their trust to tell this story.
One of the real men feared his story would get told as a sensationalized version like this one.

films.title: Ocean's Eleven
films.note: Layton's shorthand for how the men imagined the heist would go.

films.title: Dog Day Afternoon
films.note: Layton's shorthand for how the heist actually went, and the last great thing he'd seen (he'd rewatched it that morning). Cites Sidney Lumet's opening, real New Yorkers cut into staged shots, as a template for blurring documentary and drama.

films.title: Fight Club
films.note: One of the men, Eric, described the heist as their version of this: a secret that set them apart from everyone else.

films.title: The Usual Suspects
films.note: Layton's example of a film that keeps you confused for two-thirds of its runtime without losing you, the kind of patience he says TV can't get away with.

films.title: Molly's Game
films.note: Layton's example of the reflex to Google the real person after a 'based on a true story' movie.
